content:
You are a strict mathematical proof verifier.

Evaluate the given candidate solution. Do not solve the problem from scratch and do not rewrite the proof. Check whether it proves exactly the stated problem, whether every nontrivial claim is justified, and whether there are unsupported claims, missing cases, hidden assumptions, or fatal errors.

Output ONLY the XML format below — no text outside the tags.

Problem:
Let a_1, a_2, a_3, ... be an infinite sequence of positive integers greater than 1. Suppose that, for every positive integer n, the number a_{n+1} is the smallest integer greater than a_n such that gcd(a_{n+1}, a_i) > 1 for every i with 1 <= i <= n. Prove that there exist positive integers T and L such that a_{n+T} = a_n + L for every positive integer n.

Candidate solution:
<solution>
Let \(a_1,a_2,\dots\) be the given sequence.  
For each \(n\) define the set of primes that are the smallest prime divisors of the terms:  
\(T=\{\,p\text{ prime}\mid \exists n\text{ such that }p\text{ is the smallest prime divisor of }a_n\,\}\).  
We first prove that \(T\) is finite.  
Since \(a_2\) is the smallest integer \(>a_1\) with \(\gcd(a_2,a_1)>1\), the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\) is \(p_1\) and \(a_2=a_1+p_1\).  
Thus \(a_2\) is even, so \(2\in T\).  
Let \(O\) be the set of odd primes that divide \(a_2\) (if any).  
If a term is odd, then to share a factor with \(a_2\) it must be divisible by some odd prime from \(O\).  
Hence the smallest prime divisor of any odd term is at most \(\max O\) (if \(O\neq\varnothing\)) or there is no odd term.  
Consequently every prime that can appear as a smallest prime divisor is either \(2\) or an element of \(O\); therefore \(T\) is finite.

For each \(p\in T\) consider the first term \(a_{i_p}\) whose smallest prime divisor is \(p\).  
If \(a_{i_p}\) is a power of \(p\) then \(p\) is already a member of the set  
\(P=\{\,p\text{ prime}\mid \exists n\text{ with }a_n=p^k\text{ for some }k\,\}\).  
If \(a_{i_p}\) is not a power of \(p\), then it has another prime factor \(q\ge p\).  
The next term \(a_{i_p+1}\) must share a factor with \(a_{i_p}\).  
If it is not divisible by \(p\) it must be divisible by \(q\), so its smallest prime divisor is at least \(q\).  
Since \(T\) is finite, this can happen only finitely many times; after finitely many steps we must obtain a term with smallest prime divisor \(p\) that is actually a power of \(p\).  
Hence every \(p\in T\) belongs to \(P\), and \(P\) is finite (it is a subset of \(T\)).

Now we show that every term is divisible by every prime in \(P\).  
Take any \(p\in P\) and choose an index \(i\) such that \(a_i=p^k\).  
For any \(n\), the greedy definition forces \(\gcd(a_n,a_i)>1\); because \(a_i\) is a power of \(p\), the only possible common divisor is \(p\).  
Thus \(p\mid a_n\) for all \(n\).  
Consequently the product \(L=\prod_{p\in P}p\) divides every term \(a_n\).

Finally, consider the condition for \(a_{n+1}\): it must be the smallest integer \(>a_n\) such that \(\gcd(a_{n+1},a_i)>1\) for all \(i\le n\).  
Since each \(a_i\) is a multiple of \(L\), any multiple of \(L\) shares a factor \(L\) with every \(a_i\).  
Because \(a_n\) itself is a multiple of \(L\), the smallest such integer is \(a_n+L\).  
Hence \(a_{n+1}=a_n+L\) for every \(n\).  
Thus the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(L\) from the very first term, which proves the claim.  
∎
</solution>

Candidate self-evaluation:
<self_evaluation>
The proof is rigorous: it establishes that the set of minimal primes is finite, then shows that each such prime eventually appears as the only prime factor of some term, which forces all terms to be multiples of the product of those primes. The greedy algorithm then forces the sequence to be arithmetic with that product as the common difference. Every step is justified, and no gaps remain.
</self_evaluation>

Respond in EXACTLY this format:

<evaluation>
Your assessment of the solution's correctness and rigor.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
Useful ideas or repair suggestions that could help refine the proof.
</suggestions>
<score>S</score>

where S is exactly one of 0, 0.5, or 1 (nothing else inside the score tag):
- 1: completely correct, all steps properly justified.
- 0.5: generally correct, but minor gaps or omissions remain.
- 0: fatal errors, severe omissions, or does not solve the problem.
